meta.title: Debout, Stoll! — Collège Stoll d'Akono & St Joseph Minor Seminary
meta.description: SOS Collège Stoll d'Akono — a heritage in peril. The living memory of one of Cameroon's great schools, and a summons to every Stollois of yesterday, today and tomorrow.
meta.ogTitle: SOS Collège Stoll d'Akono — a heritage in peril
meta.ogDescription: Fifteen hundred pupils in 2004; fewer than two hundred today. A summons to every son and daughter of Stoll to rescue sixty years of memory, discipline and distinction.

cta.contribute: Entrust a photograph or a memory

hero.title: One hill,
hero.titleEm: three ages of a single work
hero.lede: From an Alsatian Spiritan mission to the college raised by Marist Brothers who came from Quebec: the living memory of Collège Stoll d'Akono and of the Saint Joseph Minor Seminary that prepared its ground.

racines.title: Raised upon the seminary's foundations
racines.intro: Collège Stoll did not rise from untouched ground. It stands on the very site once largely occupied by the Akono Minor Seminary — upon a mission founded by an Alsatian Spiritan and carried forward by Brothers who came from Canada. Three institutions, one patronage, one enduring soil.

eglise.title: The church, from which the whole work sprang
eglise.f1title: A cathedral deep in the bush
eglise.f1p1: Raised between 1933 and 1938 to Father Stoll's own designs and under the hand of Brother Alphonse Quémeneur, an engineer, the Church of Our Lady of the Seven Sorrows is reckoned across Cameroon and the wider region among the noblest monuments in the history of the Catholic faith. Beaver-tail tiles, brick and timber — the building craft of Alsace, transplanted into the equatorial forest.
eglise.f1p2: It was this church that gave the place its soul. The painter Atini Charles, whom Brother Lavoie discovered as he was at work painting the Akono church, would in time adorn the walls of Collège Stoll itself.
eglise.f1p3: Here Father Stoll would say, again and again: “The things of God must be handled with reverence.” Those words, incised in the Ewondo tongue, keep watch to this day over the college gate.
eglise.f1cap: Today. The ochre-and-white façade of Our Lady of the Seven Sorrows. © GESCOD / Michel Weyland
eglise.strip1: 1. The builder. Father Stoll on the mission worksite. © Spiritan Archives, Chevilly-Larue.
eglise.strip2: 2. The people of God. The central aisle on a feast day, around 1937.
eglise.strip3: 3. The façade, 1937. Our Lady of the Seven Sorrows on a feast day. © Spiritan Archives.
eglise.f2title: A child of the hill returns to consecrate it
eglise.f2p1: The photograph tells the whole story of Akono. The man who inaugurates Collège Stoll on 23 February 1969 — Mr Paul Biya, Secretary General of the Presidency of the Republic of Cameroon — had himself been a pupil of the Saint Joseph Minor Seminary of Akono, the mother house raised on this same hill. A child formed on this ground returns, now as an envoy of the State, to consecrate the new work that carries the old one forward.
eglise.f2p2: About the table, the three pillars stand united: the Cameroonian State, the Church in the person of the Apostolic Nuncio, and the Canada of the Marist Brothers in the person of the Ambassador. From seminary to college, the hill of Akono here redeems its every promise.
eglise.f2p3: Three weeks earlier, Father Stoll himself — invited from his retirement in Alsace — had answered in a letter dated 2 February 1969, moved that a college should bear his name, “a college of which he had dreamed in his years as parish priest.”
eglise.f2cap: 23 February 1969. Mr Paul Biya — once a pupil of the Akono Minor Seminary — inaugurates Collège Stoll beside the Apostolic Nuncio and the Ambassador of Canada. A period colour document — a rare archive.

figures.title: A pantheon of founders and masters
figures.intro: Seven faces, seven lives spent upon a single work — from the Alsatian Spiritan to the Quebec chronicler, from the founding archbishop to a figure cherished only in living memory, and on to a former pupil who became an artist of worldwide renown. The pantheon of those who made Stoll — and whom Stoll sent out to shine.

voix.num: IV · THE VOICE OF THE FOUNDERS
voix.title: What the chronicle handed down to us
voix.intro: The account of Brother Laurent Potvin, an eyewitness to it all, gives this history its flesh and blood. Three passages form its beating heart.

films.title: Stoll & Akono, in motion
films.intro: Heritage is not only read; it is watched, alive. These films gather the many gazes cast upon the college, the mission and its estate. Press ▶ to begin each film.

lieux.title: The estate and its stones
lieux.intro: Fourteen hectares deep in equatorial forest: the jewel of a church, the two campuses, the boarding houses, a sports complex without equal in the region. Add images here as they are recovered or newly photographed.

sources.title: Where the memory may be drawn
sources.intro: The archives, collections and films to be sought out in order to complete this record.

constat.sosEyebrow: ⚠ SOS Collège Stoll — a heritage in peril
constat.sosBig: Fifteen hundred pupils in 2004. Fewer than two hundred today.
constat.sosP: A school built for two thousand of the young has been emptying ever since the Marists departed. A heritage does not die of old age — it dies of neglect. What Stoll once formed must now, in turn, carry Stoll.
constat.sosClose: Keepers of an inheritance, builders of what comes next — the hour to act is now.
constat.title: What Stoll possessed — and what it has become
constat.leadHook: This document, filmed on the ground, lays bare the depth of the fall.
constat.leadBody: Collège Stoll was no ordinary school: it was a self-contained, modern, self-sufficient whole, at the very edge of its age.
constat.invLead: A vast material and human inheritance, now imperilled:
constat.strike: All of it once existed, functioned, formed one generation after another. All of it, today, stands on the edge of oblivion. That is why the call can wait no longer.

rejoindre.title: Those whom Stoll once moulded are now called to uphold it
rejoindre.lead: This is no longer a petition; it is a pledge. In joining the alumni network you write your name into the chain that binds the first class to those yet to come. Every membership counts; every gesture saves a little of the house.
rejoindre.actionsLead: Two ways to act, this very moment:
rejoindre.quote: “The alumni network of Collège Stoll d'Akono: heirs of one house, keepers of its future.”

contribute.title: Entrust a photograph, a memory, a forgotten figure
contribute.lead: The memory of Stoll rests in your hands. Every class photograph, every anecdote, every name half-forgotten enriches the pantheon and the gallery. Each contribution is reviewed before it is published.
contribute.name: Your name
contribute.email: Email (so that we may reach you)
contribute.kindFigure: A forgotten figure for the pantheon
contribute.storyPh: Set down the scene, the people, the year, the place…
contribute.fileHint: JPG, PNG or PDF — 8 MB maximum.
contribute.consent: I authorise the publication of this contribution on the site, with a credit to its source.
contribute.success: Your contribution now joins the review queue. We will be in touch should we need to. Thanks to you, the memory grows.
contribute.error: The submission failed. Please try again, or write to contact@debout-stoll.org.
contribute.consentRequired: Please tick the box authorising publication.
contribute.note: Reviewed by hand. Nothing is published without approval. Your details serve the alumni network alone.

credits.title: Credits & legal notice
credits.intro: This archive is a work of memory, borne by the alumni network of Collège Stoll d'Akono. It gathers documents whose rights belong to their authors and institutions; permissions to publish are being sought.
credits.sourcesBody: Spiritan Archives of Chevilly-Larue · the GESCOD brochure (© Michel Weyland) · the chronicle of Brother Laurent Potvin, “L'Afrique aux mille couleurs” (2003) · the Antheal Vision film · photographs from the alumni network.
credits.rightsBody: The images and documents remain the property of their holders. Anyone holding rights over a published item may request its correction or removal by writing to us.
credits.privacyBody: Information gathered through the forms — membership and contribution alike — serves only to sustain the alumni network and is never sold to third parties. You hold the right of access, rectification and erasure: write to contact@debout-stoll.org.
credits.editorBody: The alumni network of Collège Stoll d'Akono — contact@debout-stoll.org — debout-stoll.org.
